author: @MichaelKent-h5m
text:
Hi Robert
First of all I love all of your videos and the way that your mind works.
So I think that you could be the right person for me to ask.
Some how my mind likes to play with the magic of science and creates ways of solving problems and pieces ideas together.
Having said that I would like to offer you an opportunity to help me bring some of my ideas to life.
I hope that if they’re successful, there’s a real chance of helping change the world.
I hope that you get to read this.

author: @johnlomax563
text:
I built something similar out of wood 10 years ago. I had exactly the same idea:
A seesaw water motor that converted the back and forth motion into rotary motion. My water motor did work, but it was  relatively inefficient because of low power and high volume of water used. This idea is not new, however it was fun to build and fun to see it actually work.

author: @christmassnow3465
text: I realized that the swaying mechanism has tendency to fall back to the left (left side on the video, or your right side) as soon as the water drains. A more efficient way would be to store the water inside a tank with a syphon which empties itself as soon as the water reaches a certain level, something like the Pythagorean cup.

author: @aftonline

author: @warpspeednow
text: The Roman's did this with a water wheel but it rotated continuously turning a drive shaft. I think that's a more efficient design overall. 😊

replies.author: @BruceNJeffAreMyFlies
replies.text: While I think he just did this for the sake of curiosity (which is awesome), there are advantages to his design. Mainly, you can get more torque from Roberts design. A roman water wheel is essentially a paddle that catches some of the energy, from the water moving, while this design stores the energy of the water falling, and releases it all at once.

replies.author: @warpspeednow
replies.text: @BruceNJeffAreMyFlies  The torque is relative to the size of the wheel and the applied force. The Roman's then had gears to transfer the power. Roberts design is flawed since the water has nowhere to go once it leaves the tube.

replies.author: @BruceNJeffAreMyFlies
@warpspeednow
replies.text: @BruceNJeffAreMyFlies  oh, really. Get a bigger stream or build a dam to supply the requiredwater volume. The Roman's were doing it 2000 years ago. They were a lot smarter than you. The flip flop unit will do very little in the way of producing power / torque in any case and is not in use nor has it ever been in use.

author: @DerekWalsh-l4i
text: Although you say it does, the final version does NOT produce continuous rotation of the large cog wheel. It always moves in one direction, but starts and stops in time with the rocking of the water tube., And until that cog rotates continuously this makes it pretty useless as a power source. An interesting project, but needing a lot more work to make it into anything worthwhile.

author: @peterclancy3653
text: Back in the 70s a rainfall measuring device called a pluviograph used a tilting bucket system you have there to measure rainfall. These had a syphon system to empty the catching funnel( machined 8”) and this  emptied into the bucket system whichever caused the bucket to tip. Like you show in your video. A magnetic reed switch would send an impulse to a time/ rain cart recorder to record the rain event. These were spring clock operated recorders which could run for up to a year without winding. Had a Negator spring to power the clock. The recorders were made by leopold and Stevens from Canada

author: @charleslyster1681
text: The thing you started with is called in English a ‘doser’. I built one years ago to distribute water onto a reedbed in a series of floods rather than a steady but limited trickle (this was based on my dad’s description of a wartime shipyard toilet where a ten holer had a trench under it and a doser at the end which slowly filled and tipped every 20 minutes or so. The apprentices trick at a busy time was to place burning newspaper on the water just as it tipped, then run). But what you have built, though intriguing, simply appears to be a needlessly complex and inefficient water wheel…

author: @CitizenSmith50

author: @KeithOlson
text:
I looked into those a while back and the best idea I came up with was to use it as a 'capacitor' as part of a gravity generator.  Basically, after the container fills up, it tips into a bucket on a chain which begins to descend because of the sudden extra weight, turning a generator.  When the bucket gets to the bottom, the bucket gets tipped over, the water pours out, the counterweight pulls the bucket back up to the top and the cycle starts all over again.  Unlike a system like yours which uses small amounts of water to move all of the time, waiting until there is a lot of weight means friction/etc. won't have very much affect on the system.  (It would be quieter, too.)

Cheers!

author: @palarious
text:
Now, expand your two rocker compartments into a disc of such compartments, and you could have a rotary engine, essentially.

That said, the brilliance of this mechanism isn't to product circular motion, which can be done more efficiently other ways, but in converting water into up and down motion.

This is how they made huge forge bellows and hammers all throughout Britain in the very earliest days of the Industrial revolution, before steam.

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
would a spring on that outer gear act as a capacitor?  allowing jerky input to have a smoother output on whatever clock/motor mechanism it'd be attached to..  
maybe a mechanism ike what's used on cuckoo/grandfather clocks to smooth the timing from the weights to the spring..  
the more gears, the more loss.  what's the best output?  lifting a weight for potential energy storage?   
if that's a good path, would lifting a weight just less than the weight of the water in the bucket by a few inches each time have less loss in the system, and then it's all recouped when the lifted weight is dropped?  so many questions. :-)

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text: I don't know what it is , or what it is used for - but it surely works...Of course , garden ornament can be improved , as someone below suggested , by stacking it up vertically (pendulums) , or axially , at different , slightly overlapping angles (for smooth operation) and by using opposing ratchet mechanisms . Translating pendulum movement into rotation is only needed , if you want something else to rotate , otherwise , it can be translated into linear motion ,or , if you want to generate some electricity , just glue on magnets , at the ends of the pendulum pipe and put coils on each side of pipe end travel path. It can be cool conversation starter peace , at barbecues (where it is allowed) , or garden parties .

text: As always, surprising me with things that already surprise me and I stare when I see them. I would also try that prototype with a kind of spring and a small serpentine coil that you made in 2037 and also piezoelectrics where the tube hits. Did you know that brown sugar has piezoelectric properties? I'm about to try it

author: @billy-go9kx
text: I think a counter display to show how many times it has operated.  Put in a window and people would stand there amazed.  They would proclaim when they saw it turn to 1000, 10000, 1000000 and so on to their friends.  A novelty for the masses.

author: @TheMarcusrobbins
text: I think you've lost it mate 😆😆🤣 But I love it

author: @Jack__________
text: Narrow that funnel outlet down and widen the inlet to create a more consistent flow of the water. That might compensate for an uneven rate of the original water capture. Just a thought.

replies.author: @BruceNJeffAreMyFlies
replies.text:
A better idea would be adding a simple barrier, like a plate, that can be used to adjust the flow. Something like the ball inside a ball valve, or even just a little sliding sheet of plastic that moves back and forth.
This would allow adjustments for different flow rates, with minimal changes to design.

author: @reedhymas7730
text: Neat device, but straight water wheel seems like it would be more efficient and with fewer things to break

replies.author: @McWhisperASMR
replies.text: He stated it's to be used for a location where there is very little flow that wouldn't be able to turn a water wheel.

author: @justtinkering6713
text:
Turn a trickle into a power source.
The original version fills the whole tube until it is  unbalanced, then dumps the whole load. In the case of an AC alternator you don't need the one way gear conversion. Back and forth is just fine.
